biography:
Clockwork Cattle

Large construct, unaligned

A malfunctioning clockwork construct created by Flick Cogsworth. Looks like a brass-and-copper cow with glowing blue eye-lenses and steam vents. Not evil — just broken. Gears whir and click inside its barrel-shaped torso, and jets of hot steam occasionally shoot from its nostrils.

Armor Class 14 (natural armor)

Hit Points 32 (5d10 + 5)

Speed 40 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
16 (+3)8 (−1)12 (+1)3 (−4)10 (+0)1 (−5)

Damage Immunities poison

Damage Vulnerabilities lightning

Condition Immunities charmed, exhaustion, frightened, paralyzed, petrified, poisoned

Senses darkvision 60 ft., passive Perception 10

Languages —

Challenge 1 (200 XP)

Traits

Arcane Weak Point. A character who succeeds on a DC 15 Intelligence (Investigation or Arcana) check as an action can identify a glowing arcane node on the construct. The next attack against the cattle that hits the node (called shot, no penalty) stuns the cattle until the end of its next turn.

Stampede Charge (Recharge 5–6). If the cattle moves at least 20 feet straight toward a target and then hits it with a gore attack on the same turn, the target takes an extra 14 (4d6) bludgeoning damage and must succeed on a DC 13 Strength saving throw or be knocked prone.

Actions

Gore. Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 10 (2d6 + 3) piercing damage.

DM Notes

The Clockwork Cattle are not evil — they are malfunctioning constructs. If players find creative solutions (like using lightning damage to short-circuit them, or using Flick’s shutdown codes), reward them with full XP. The Arcane Weak Point mechanic rewards Investigation-focused characters. Run the cattle as panicked animals — they charge in straight lines and try to flee when stunned. A herd of 4–6 creates a chaotic low-level encounter.

biography:
Sir Corwin Ashford

Medium humanoid (human), chaotic good

The “Bandit Prince of Emerald Pass.” A disgraced knight turned Robin Hood figure. Charismatic, honorable, fights with style. He wears battered but well-maintained armor adorned with a faded golden eagle — the crest of a noble house that disowned him. A torn golden invitation to the Heroic Games was found among his belongings.

Armor Class 18 (chain mail + shield)

Hit Points 78 (12d8 + 24)

Speed 30 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
16 (+3)14 (+2)14 (+2)12 (+1)13 (+1)16 (+3)

Saving Throws Str +5, Con +4

Skills Athletics +5, Persuasion +5, Insight +3

Senses passive Perception 11

Languages Common

Challenge 4 (1,100 XP)

Traits

Battlemaster Maneuvers (4/Short Rest). Superiority Dice: 4d8. DC 13. Corwin knows: Riposte, Disarming Attack, Menacing Attack, Goading Attack.

Disarming Attack. When Corwin hits with a weapon attack, he can add 1d8 to the damage. The target must make a DC 13 Strength saving throw or drop one held item.

Menacing Attack. When Corwin hits with a weapon attack, he can add 1d8 to the damage. The target must make a DC 13 Wisdom saving throw or be frightened until the end of Corwin’s next turn.

Goading Attack. When Corwin hits with a weapon attack, he can add 1d8 to the damage. The target must make a DC 13 Wisdom saving throw or have disadvantage on attacks against targets other than Corwin until the end of his next turn.

Surrender Threshold. Corwin surrenders at 19 HP or below (25%). He drops his sword, raises his hands. He does not fight to kill — all attacks deal non-lethal damage if a PC drops to 0.

Actions

Multiattack. Corwin makes two longsword attacks.

Longsword. Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (1d8 + 3) slashing damage, or 8 (1d10 + 3) if used two-handed.

Handaxe. Ranged Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) slashing damage.

Reactions

Riposte. When a creature misses Corwin with a melee attack, he can use his reaction to make a melee weapon attack against that creature, adding 1d8 to the damage.

Parry. Corwin adds 2 to his AC against one melee attack that would hit him. He must see the attacker and be wielding a melee weapon.

DM Notes

Corwin is a sympathetic villain — he robs supply wagons to feed displaced villagers. He fights with honor and style, using Menacing Attack to frighten weaker PCs and Goading Attack to draw attacks to himself. He always surrenders before dying. The torn golden invitation is a critical clue to Phase 2. If the party spares him, he becomes a valuable ally and information source. In combat, he leads his 8 bandits from the front, opening with Menacing Attack to frighten casters, then using Riposte to punish melee attackers. He never targets unconscious PCs.

biography:
Gynosphinx — Labyrinth Guardian

Large monstrosity, lawful neutral

The guardian of the Labyrinth of Trials beneath the Grand Arena. This ancient sphinx has golden fur, eagle wings, and an inscrutable expression carved from centuries of watching mortals fail. Geometric golden patterns — matching those found throughout Aurelius — are etched into its flanks. It follows specific combat rules: fights for only 3 rounds if riddles are failed, then resets.

Armor Class 17 (natural armor)

Hit Points 105 (14d10 + 28)

Speed 40 ft., fly 60 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
18 (+4)15 (+2)14 (+2)18 (+4)18 (+4)18 (+4)

Skills Arcana +8, History +8, Perception +8, Religion +8

Senses truesight 120 ft., passive Perception 18

Languages Common, Sphinx

Challenge 8 (3,900 XP)

Traits

Inscrutable. The sphinx is immune to any effect that would sense its emotions or read its thoughts, as well as any divination spell that it refuses. Wisdom (Insight) checks against the sphinx have disadvantage.

Magic Weapons. The sphinx’s weapon attacks are magical.

Three Riddles. The sphinx poses three riddles. DC 15 Intelligence for Riddle 1, DC 17 for Riddle 2, DC 20 for Riddle 3. Success unlocks the door. Failure means the sphinx attacks for 3 rounds then resets.

Riddle 1 (DC 15). “I am always coming but never arrive. What am I?” Answer: Tomorrow.

Riddle 2 (DC 17). “I have cities but no houses, forests but no trees, and water but no fish. What am I?” Answer: A map.

Riddle 3 (DC 20). “The more you take, the more you leave behind. What am I?” Answer: Footsteps.

Actions

Multiattack. The sphinx makes two claw attacks.

Claw. Melee Weapon Attack: +8 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 13 (2d8 + 4) slashing damage.

DM Notes

The sphinx is a reskinned gynosphinx with a unique riddle-gate mechanic. It does not fight to kill. If the party fails the riddles, it attacks for exactly 3 rounds, then returns to its pedestal and the encounter resets. Players can attempt the riddles again after a short rest. The riddles can be solved by the players (not just ability checks) — if a player gives the correct answer at the table, auto-success regardless of the check. The sphinx speaks in a calm, measured voice and shows no emotion regardless of the outcome. It has been here for centuries and will be here for centuries more.

biography:
Bron Ironfist

Medium humanoid (dwarf), lawful good

Leader of the Iron Vanguard. A stocky dwarf fighter-champion with arms like tree trunks and a beard braided with iron rings. Gruff exterior, heart of gold. He and his party are the beloved previous champions of the Heroic Games — secretly frozen in stasis pods beneath Solhaven Palace.

Armor Class 18 (plate)

Hit Points 90 (12d8 + 36)

Speed 25 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
18 (+4)10 (+0)16 (+3)10 (+0)12 (+1)14 (+2)

Saving Throws Str +7, Con +6

Skills Athletics +7, Intimidation +5

Senses darkvision 60 ft., passive Perception 11

Languages Common, Dwarvish

Challenge 5 (1,800 XP)

Traits

Dwarven Resilience. Advantage on saving throws against poison, and resistance to poison damage.

Improved Critical. Bron scores a critical hit on a roll of 19 or 20.

Actions

Multiattack. Bron makes two warhammer attacks.

Warhammer. Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 8 (1d8 + 4) bludgeoning damage.

Second Wind (1/Rest). As a bonus action, Bron regains 1d10 + 5 hit points.

DM Notes

Bron is the heart of the Iron Vanguard and a key emotional anchor for Phase 2. When found in stasis, he is mid-swing with his warhammer, frozen in time. If freed, he is disoriented but quickly rallies. He is fiercely protective of his team and will insist on freeing all of them before leaving. In combat as an ally, he anchors the front line, uses Second Wind early, and prioritizes protecting squishier party members. His Improved Critical makes him a reliable damage dealer. He calls everyone “lad” or “lass” regardless of their actual age.

Sylara Moonwhisper

Iron Vanguard’s wizard. A serene elven divination wizard who always seems to know what’s going to happen — except when it mattered most. Tall and willowy with silver-white hair and pale violet eyes that occasionally flash with prescient light. Found frozen in stasis beneath Solhaven Palace with an expression of stunned surprise — she didn’t see it coming.

Sylara is the Iron Vanguard’s tactical brain. As an ally, she stays at range and uses Fireball for groups and Counterspell to shut down enemy casters. She uses Portent defensively — forcing enemy saves to fail or ensuring ally saves succeed. She is deeply shaken by having been captured without forewarning and becomes obsessed with understanding Valerius’s chronomancy. She speaks softly and rarely makes eye contact, but her tactical advice is impeccable. If Mage Armor is cast, her AC rises to 16.

biography:
Torq

Medium humanoid (half-orc), chaotic good

Iron Vanguard’s barbarian. A gentle half-orc poet-barbarian who writes terrible poetry and fights with terrifying efficiency. Broad-shouldered with grey-green skin and surprisingly kind eyes. Found frozen with an unfinished poem crumpled in his hand. The poem reads: “The axe falls like autumn leaves / But softer, I think, than—”

Armor Class 14 (unarmored defense)

Hit Points 95 (10d12 + 30)

Speed 40 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
18 (+4)14 (+2)16 (+3)8 (−1)12 (+1)10 (+0)

Saving Throws Str +7, Con +6

Skills Athletics +7, Perception +4, Survival +4

Senses passive Perception 14

Languages Common, Orc

Challenge 5 (1,800 XP)

Traits

Rage (3/Day). As a bonus action, Torq enters a rage for 1 minute. While raging: +2 melee damage, resistance to bludgeoning/piercing/slashing, advantage on Strength checks and saves. Cannot cast spells.

Bear Totem. While raging, Torq has resistance to all damage except psychic.

Relentless Endurance (1/Day). When reduced to 0 HP but not killed, Torq drops to 1 HP instead.

Danger Sense. Advantage on Dexterity saving throws against effects Torq can see.

Reckless Attack. At the start of his turn, Torq can choose to attack recklessly. He gains advantage on melee attack rolls during that turn, but attack rolls against him have advantage until his next turn.

Actions

Multiattack. Torq makes two greataxe attacks.

Greataxe. Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 10 (1d12 + 4) slashing damage, plus 2 while raging.

DM Notes

Torq is comic relief with surprising depth. He always opens combat by raging (bonus action) then using Reckless Attack for advantage on both greataxe swings. With Bear Totem, he has resistance to all damage except psychic while raging — making him an exceptional tank. Relentless Endurance means he effectively has to be “killed” twice. He recites terrible poetry between attacks. Sample lines: “Your skull is like a melon / Round and full of seeds of thought.” He is deeply loyal to Bron and will not leave without the whole team.

Pip fights smart — she uses Cunning Action to Hide every turn, then attacks with advantage for Sneak Attack. With +10 Stealth (expertise), she is almost impossible to find in dim light. She targets isolated enemies and avoids fair fights. Outside of combat, she compulsively steals trinkets (never anything important) and offers them as gifts to PCs she likes. She is fiercely loyal to the Iron Vanguard family and especially protective of Bron, whom she calls “Da” (much to his embarrassment).

biography:
Gilded Sentinel (CR 7)

Large construct, unaligned

A towering golden construct in the shape of an idealized humanoid. Geometric golden patterns cover its surface and pulse with warm light. Created by Valerius to guard key locations. Stands 9 feet tall and weighs over a ton. Its face is a featureless golden mask with two burning amber eyes.

Armor Class 18 (natural armor)

Hit Points 142 (15d10 + 60)

Speed 30 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
18 (+4)8 (−1)18 (+4)7 (−2)10 (+0)3 (−4)

Saving Throws Str +7, Con +7

Damage Immunities poison

Damage Resistances Bludgeoning, Piercing, and Slashing from Nonmagical Attacks

Damage Vulnerabilities thunder

Condition Immunities charmed, exhaustion, frightened, paralyzed, poisoned

Senses blindsight 10 ft., darkvision 60 ft., passive Perception 10

Languages understands Common but can’t speak

Challenge 7 (2,900 XP)

Traits

Regeneration. The Sentinel regains 10 hit points at the start of its turn if it has at least 1 HP. Thunder damage suppresses this regeneration for 1 round.

Immutable Form. The Sentinel is immune to any spell or effect that would alter its form.

Death Voice. When the Sentinel is destroyed, a pre-recorded message from Archduke Valerius plays: “You were warned. This did not have to happen.” The message is audible within 300 feet.

Actions

Multiattack. The Sentinel makes two slam attacks.

Slam. Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 15 (2d10 + 4) bludgeoning damage.

Misty Step (1/Day). As a bonus action, the Sentinel magically teleports up to 30 feet to an unoccupied space it can see.

DM Notes

The Gilded Sentinel is the signature enemy of the campaign. Its regeneration (10 HP/round) makes it feel relentless — the key is thunder damage, which shuts it down for a round. Shatter is the obvious counter. The Death Voice is a powerful narrative tool: every time the party destroys one, Valerius’s voice reminds them he knows they’re coming. Use Misty Step to surprise players who think they’re safe at range. The Sentinel fights methodically, targeting the nearest enemy and moving to the next when one falls. It does not retreat.

DM Notes

Golden Guards are designed to reward tactical play. Their AC 20 with Shield of Faith is punishing, but the buff only lasts 10 minutes and is once per day — if the party can wait it out or Dispel Magic it, the AC drops to 18. The Coordination ability means clustered guards are devastating (advantage on all attacks), but isolated guards lose their edge. Smart parties will use area effects to scatter them, then pick them off. Most guards are not evil — they are soldiers following orders. Some can be talked down, especially if Captain Vayne has been turned.

She has luminous golden skin, white-blonde hair, and eyes that glow with soft divine light. She serves as the party’s guide to Aurelius in Phase 2 and may become a reluctant antagonist in Phase 3. Her character arc is central to the campaign’s moral themes.

Elara is one of the most important NPCs in the campaign. In Phase 2, she is warm, helpful, and genuinely kind — she believes Valerius is building paradise. The DC 22 Persuasion check to turn her is intentionally high but not impossible — evidence from the stasis pods, testimony from the Iron Vanguard, or invoking her divine oath can grant advantage. biography:
Duplicate King Thalric

Medium construct (temporal duplicate), lawful neutral

A perfect temporal copy of King Thalric Greenbough, created by Valerius to rule Verdaine in the real king’s absence. Speaks, acts, and looks exactly like the king — but cannot produce specific personal memories. Answers policy questions flawlessly but fails on emotional detail. The real king is frozen in a stasis pod beneath Castle Greenbough. When damaged, the Duplicate’s form flickers with golden light, briefly revealing the clockwork temporal matrix beneath its skin.

Armor Class 15 (temporal armor)

Hit Points 150 (20d8 + 60)

Speed 30 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
10 (+0)16 (+3)16 (+3)20 (+5)14 (+2)18 (+4)

Saving Throws Int +9, Wis +6, Cha +8

Skills Deception +8, History +9, Persuasion +8

Condition Immunities charmed, frightened

Senses passive Perception 12

Languages Common, Elvish, Dwarvish, Sylvan

Challenge 12 (8,400 XP)

Traits

Magic Resistance. The Duplicate has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

Chronal Defense. The Duplicate has advantage on initiative rolls.

Temporal Shell. When the Duplicate is reduced to 0 HP, its body freezes in time for 1 round, gaining 50 temporary hit points. If these are depleted or after 1 round, the shell shatters and the Duplicate is truly destroyed.

Actions

Multiattack. The Duplicate makes two Chronal Bolt attacks.

Chronal Bolt. Ranged Spell Attack: +9 to hit, range 120 ft., one target. Hit: 22 (4d8 + 4) force damage.

Slow (At Will). Spell save DC 17 Wisdom. Up to 6 creatures within 120 ft. On a failed save, the target’s speed is halved, it takes a −2 penalty to AC and Dexterity saves, and it can’t use reactions. On its turn, it can use either an action or a bonus action, not both.

Time Stop (1/Day). The Duplicate briefly stops the flow of time for everyone but itself. It takes 1d4 + 1 turns in a row, during which other creatures are frozen.

Reactions

Temporal Flicker (3/Day). When targeted by an attack, the Duplicate teleports up to 15 feet and imposes disadvantage on that attack roll.

DM Notes

The Duplicate King is a puzzle boss as much as a combat encounter. It opens with Slow (at will!) to debilitate the party, then uses Chronal Bolt from range. Temporal Flicker makes it frustratingly evasive — three uses mean the first three big hits might miss. Time Stop is its panic button: it uses those free turns to reposition, cast Slow again, and set up Chronal Bolt volleys. The Temporal Shell at 0 HP means the party has to “kill” it twice (50 temp HP). The real challenge is exposing the Duplicate before combat — ask it about the king’s childhood pet, his wedding vows, or his mother’s name. It answers with technically correct but emotionally hollow responses that Archdruid Eira immediately recognizes as wrong.

Medium humanoid (elf), neutral good

She was the first to realize the king had been replaced. She brings powerful druid magic to the final battle as a key ally. Her testimony about the Duplicate King is one of the most emotionally impactful handouts in the campaign.

DM Notes

Eira is the party’s most powerful ally in Phase 3. As an NPC ally, she prioritizes support: Healing Word for downed PCs, Call Lightning for sustained damage, and Dispel Magic to counter enemy effects. She uses Stoneskin on the party’s tank before big fights and Wall of Thorns to control battlefields. Heal (70 HP, no roll) is her emergency button. Transport via Plants allows fast travel between locations with large trees. She is calm, wise, and heartbroken — she has known Thalric for over a century and immediately recognized the Duplicate’s emotional hollowness. Her testimony handout reads: “I asked him about the day his daughter was born. He told me the date, the weight, the name. But he did not smile. Thalric always smiles when he speaks of Lily.”

This is the mid-tier Sentinel variant. Mechanically identical to the CR 7 version but with +4 proficiency bonus instead of +3, resulting in higher attack bonus (+8 vs +7) and save DCs. Smart parties will use thunder damage (Shatter, Thunderwave) to suppress regeneration on multiple Sentinels at once. The cryptic Death Voice message “Return what was taken” refers to the stolen essence of the frozen champions. If the party has already freed champions, the message hits differently.

The most powerful Sentinel variant. Guards the Anchor of Space in the Sunken Vault beneath Ironhaven Harbor. This Sentinel is larger than its lesser counterparts, standing nearly 12 feet tall with more elaborate golden filigree and eyes that burn with intense amber fire. It has enhanced regeneration, multiple teleportation abilities, and the terrifying Spatial Shunt power that can briefly banish a creature from reality.

Immutable Form. Immune to any spell or effect that would alter its form.

Actions

Multiattack. The Sentinel makes three slam attacks.

DM Notes

This is the apex predator of the Sentinel line. Three slam attacks at +9 for 16 damage each is brutal (48 average per round). Regeneration of 15 HP/round means it heals back almost a full slam’s worth of damage every turn. Spatial Shunt is the real threat — removing the party’s tank or healer for a full round can be devastating. The Sentinel uses Dimension Door to chase fleeing parties and Misty Step (3/day) to close gaps or escape area effects. Thunder damage is still the key weakness. The Sunken Vault encounter pairs this Sentinel with rising water and collapsing architecture for a truly memorable set piece. It guards the Anchor of Space — destroying the anchor causes a brief spatial distortion that deals 4d10 force damage to everything within 30 feet.

Time Echo

Medium undead, neutral

Golden temporal copies of the player characters, summoned from the Mirror Gallery. Shimmering silhouettes that fight with echoes of the party’s own abilities. They look like golden-glass reflections of each PC — same height, same posture, same fighting stance, but translucent and flickering with temporal energy. Deliberately fragile (20 HP each) — the encounter is emotional, not lethal. Destroying the corresponding mirror instantly destroys the Echo.

Amorphous. The Echo can move through a space as narrow as 1 inch without squeezing.

DM Notes

This encounter is about emotional impact, not difficulty. Each Echo mirrors a specific PC perfectly — describe it using that PC’s mannerisms, stance, and weapon. The Echo of the barbarian holds its axe the same way. The Echo of the wizard traces the same arcane gestures. Players fighting themselves is inherently unsettling. The Mirror Link mechanic gives clever players an instant-kill option: shatter the mirror (AC 13, 10 HP) instead of fighting the Echo. The mirrors are arranged around the gallery — players can see their own reflections moving independently before the Echoes step out. Spawn one Echo per PC. If a PC destroys their own Echo, have it mouth their name silently before shattering.

Medium humanoid (human), lawful evil

The campaign’s BBEG. A 17th-level Chronurgy Wizard who has spent 14 years engineering a ritual to freeze the entire Tri-Kingdoms in a single perfect moment — no death, no disease, no change, no choice. He genuinely believes he is saving the world. Silver-streaked dark hair, immaculate cream-and-gold robes, surrounded by swirling golden essence. His eyes glow with temporal energy. He speaks with calm, measured certainty — the voice of a man who has seen every possible future and chosen this one.

Additional Spells: Disintegrate (DC 20 Dex, 10d6+40 force), Chain Lightning (DC 20 Dex, 10d8 lightning), Meteor Swarm (1/day, DC 20 Dex, 40d6), Globe of Invulnerability (1/day).

Actions

Multiattack. Valerius makes three Chronal Bolt attacks, or two Chronal Bolt attacks and casts one spell.

Chronal Bolt. Ranged Spell Attack: +12 to hit, range 120 ft., one target. Hit: 19 (3d8 + 6) force damage.

Legendary Actions (Phase 2 Only, 3/Round)

Temporal Rewind (1 Action, 3/Day total). Valerius negates the last instance of damage dealt to him, restoring up to 30 HP. Accompanied by the sound of a clock running backward.

Essence Burst (2 Actions). 60-ft. cone. Each creature: DC 18 Dexterity save, 36 (8d8) radiant damage on a failed save, half on success. Visible as a wave of golden light filled with ghostly faces.

Summon Time Echo (3 Actions, 2/Day). Valerius summons a Time Echo of a previous boss within 30 ft: d4: 1=Wight (30 HP), 2=Bandit Prince (60 HP), 3=Gilded Sentinel (70 HP), 4=Duplicate King (75 HP). Echoes have half HP and last 3 rounds.

Lair Actions (Initiative 20)

Gravity Inversion. 20-ft square within 120 ft. DC 16 Dex save or “fall” 30 ft up (3d6 bludgeoning) then 30 ft down (3d6 bludgeoning).

Time Pool. 15-ft radius within 120 ft. DC 16 Con save or 18 (4d8) necrotic damage + 1 level of Exhaustion.

Frozen Moment. One creature within 120 ft. DC 17 Wis save or Paralyzed until end of its next turn.

Death and Final Choice

At 0 HP Valerius falls, incapacitated but conscious. The ritual shatters, stored essence erupts, all stasis pods shatter, champions awaken. Players choose: Kill (clean ending), Spare/Imprison (trial, sequel hook), Use Stasis on Him (poetic justice).

DM Notes

This is a two-phase boss fight designed to escalate dramatically. In Phase 1, Valerius is restrained and philosophical — he uses Wall of Force to separate the party, Slow to debilitate them, and Hold Person to neutralize threats, all while explaining his vision. He is genuinely trying to convince the party. Counterspell at 7th level shuts down almost any spell. Shield makes him temporarily AC 23. In Phase 2, the mask drops completely. Legendary Actions make him feel relentless — Temporal Rewind is psychologically devastating (the party deals a massive hit and watches it rewind), Essence Burst punishes clustering, and Summon Time Echo creates chaos. Lair Actions on initiative 20 add constant environmental pressure. Time Pool is the most dangerous (exhaustion stacks are deadly). The Sanctuary mechanic means the party MUST destroy the Anchors before they can touch him — this creates a frantic multi-objective encounter. Run his dialogue throughout: “I have seen ten thousand futures. In nine thousand of them, everyone you love dies screaming. I am offering you the one where they don’t.”

biography:
Mayor Tilda Bramblethorn

Small humanoid (halfling), lawful good

The mayor of Oakhaven. A no-nonsense halfling grandmother who runs the town with warmth and efficiency. Her office always smells like cinnamon. She wears a practical wool dress, a chain of office that’s slightly too big for her, and reading spectacles perched on the end of her nose. She knows every citizen by name and their grandmother’s name. Quest-giver for Phase 1’s opening adventures.

Armor Class 10

Hit Points 9 (2d6 + 2)

Speed 25 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
8 (−1)12 (+1)12 (+1)14 (+2)16 (+3)16 (+3)

Skills Insight +5, Persuasion +5

Senses passive Perception 13

Languages Common, Halfling

Challenge 0 (10 XP)

Traits

Natural Authority. Tilda has advantage on Charisma (Persuasion) checks when dealing with Oakhaven citizens.

DM Notes

Tilda is the party’s introduction to the campaign world. She is warm, practical, and efficient — a perfect quest-giver who makes the players feel welcome while clearly communicating stakes. She runs the Oakhaven town council from a cozy office above the general store. She offers the party their first quests: the Clockwork Cattle, the Wine-Cellar Wight, and the Emerald Pass Bandits. She pays fairly, provides useful information, and always has fresh-baked cookies. She is not a combatant under any circumstances — if threatened, every citizen of Oakhaven will defend her. She serves as a moral compass and a reminder of what the party is fighting to protect.

biography:
Flick Cogsworth

Small humanoid (gnome), chaotic neutral

Oakhaven’s resident gnome inventor. Creator of the Clockwork Cattle (and their problems). His workshop is a converted barn that is constantly smoking, clanking, and occasionally on fire. Flick is barely three feet tall with wild copper hair that stands on end (from static, he claims, though it’s probably fear). His fingers are perpetually stained with grease and his eyebrows have been burned off so many times they’ve given up growing back. Eager to help but his solutions often create new problems.

Armor Class 11

Hit Points 7 (2d6)

Speed 25 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
8 (−1)12 (+1)10 (+0)18 (+4)10 (+0)12 (+1)

Skills Arcana +6, Investigation +6

Senses darkvision 60 ft., passive Perception 10

Languages Common, Gnomish

Challenge 0 (10 XP)

Traits

Tinker. Flick can spend 1 hour and 10 gp of materials to construct a Tiny clockwork device (AC 5, 1 HP). Examples include a clockwork toy, a fire starter, or a music box.

Artificer’s Lore. Proficiency bonus doubled for History checks related to magic items, alchemical objects, or technological devices.

DM Notes

Flick is comic relief with a heart of gold. He created the Clockwork Cattle as automated farm helpers, but a lightning strike scrambled their control crystals. He has shutdown codes that work... sometimes. He offers to help the party with gadgets that are creative but unreliable (50% chance of working as intended, 25% chance of doing nothing, 25% chance of spectacular malfunction). In later phases, Flick’s understanding of clockwork constructs becomes crucial — he can identify weak points in Gilded Sentinels if consulted. He speaks very fast, interrupts himself constantly, and refers to explosions as “energetic disassembly events.”

biography:
Margaux Delacroix

Medium humanoid (human), lawful neutral

Owner of the Delacroix Vineyard, 2 miles south of Oakhaven. Sophisticated and particular — she wears impeccable clothing even while inspecting the vines, and her silver hair is always perfectly coiffed. Her cellar extends deep into the hillside, accidentally breaching an ancient burial chamber (the Wine-Cellar Wight quest). The vintage the party saves becomes a recurring plot device throughout the campaign.

Armor Class 11

Hit Points 11 (2d8 + 2)

Speed 30 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
10 (+0)12 (+1)12 (+1)14 (+2)14 (+2)16 (+3)

Skills Persuasion +5, Insight +4

Senses passive Perception 12

Languages Common, Elvish

Challenge 1/8 (25 XP)

Traits

Wine Expertise. Margaux has advantage on Intelligence checks related to wines, brewing, and vineyard management.

DM Notes

Margaux is the quest-giver for the Wine-Cellar Wight adventure. She discovered her cellar expansion broke through into an ancient burial chamber and now undead are terrorizing her workers. She offers the party a generous reward (50 gp each plus a case of her finest vintage). The Delacroix Red vintage the party receives becomes a recurring item — it’s served at the Heroic Games in Phase 2, found in Valerius’s personal collection in Phase 3, and can be given to freed champions as a comfort. Margaux is proud, shrewd, and brooks no nonsense, but she’s also genuinely grateful and becomes a lifelong friend if the party helps her. She has strong opinions about everything and will judge the party’s table manners.

Gareth “The Blade” Lumley

Medium humanoid (human), chaotic good

Leader (by his own declaration) of the Lucky Leopards, a comic-relief rival adventuring party. His business card reads “LUKKY LEPPARDS — Advenchurers for Hire.” Enthusiastic but utterly incompetent. He wears slightly-too-large armor with a crudely painted leopard on the breastplate. His greatsword is “optimistically named” — it’s actually a standard longsword he uses without any proper training. The Leopards appear throughout the campaign as bumbling foils who somehow keep surviving.

DM Notes

Gareth and the Lucky Leopards are the campaign’s recurring comic relief. They show up at the worst possible moments: trying to clear the same dungeon the party is in, entering the Heroic Games with disastrous results, and eventually getting captured in Phase 3 (requiring rescue). Gareth is genuinely kind-hearted and enthusiastic — he just has no talent whatsoever. The Dramatic Flourish ability exists purely for comedy: there’s a 55% chance he drops his sword every time he tries to look cool. The other Lucky Leopards are: Nibbles (a halfling “wizard” who only knows Prestidigitation), Chunk (a half-orc who is afraid of blood), and Whisper (an elf ranger who is extremely loud). Despite everything, they somehow keep surviving. They are loved by tables everywhere.

biography:
Sable

Medium humanoid (human), chaotic good

Leader of the Silver Crows, the underground resistance movement opposing Valerius. Cool, competent, and always three steps ahead. She wears dark practical clothing, keeps her black hair cropped short, and has a thin scar across her left cheek from a Golden Guard’s blade. She provides the party with the Operation Daybreak mission board and serves as their primary intelligence source throughout Phase 3. Her real name is unknown — “Sable” is the only name she answers to.

Armor Class 15 (studded leather)

Hit Points 65 (10d8 + 20)

Speed 30 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
10 (+0)16 (+3)14 (+2)16 (+3)14 (+2)14 (+2)

Saving Throws Dex +6, Int +6

Skills Stealth +9 (expertise), Deception +5, Insight +5, Investigation +6

Senses passive Perception 12

Languages Common, Thieves’ Cant, Elvish

Challenge 5 (1,800 XP)

Traits

Silver Crows Leader. Sable leads the underground resistance network. She has contacts in every major city and can arrange safe houses, disguises, and intelligence.

Sneak Attack (3d6). Once per turn, Sable deals an extra 10 (3d6) damage when she hits with a finesse or ranged weapon and has advantage or an ally within 5 ft.

Evasion. No damage on successful Dex save for half, half on failure.

Actions

Shortsword. Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) piercing damage.

Hand Crossbow. Ranged Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, range 30/120 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) piercing damage.

Cunning Action. As a bonus action, Sable can Dash, Disengage, or Hide.

DM Notes

Sable is the party’s spymaster in Phase 3. She runs the Silver Crows resistance from a rotating network of safe houses. She provides the Operation Daybreak mission board: a cork board with pinned notes, maps, and intelligence reports that organizes all of Phase 3’s missions. She is calm under pressure, emotionally guarded, and rarely smiles — but she cares deeply about the people Valerius has frozen. In combat, she fights smart: using Cunning Action to Hide, then attacking with Sneak Attack from stealth. She never engages in a fair fight if she can avoid it. She has a personal vendetta against Madame Voss, who captured and froze Sable’s sister. If the party rescues her sister from the stasis pods, Sable’s composure cracks for the first and only time in the campaign.

biography:
Madame Lucretia Voss

Medium humanoid (human), lawful evil

Director of the Preservation Society, Valerius’s chief lieutenant. Runs the surveillance operation at Heroes’ Rest, manages the stasis pod infrastructure, and handles public relations. A true believer who is more ruthless than Valerius himself. She wears elegant dark clothing, keeps her silver-streaked auburn hair in a severe bun, and speaks with precise, clipped diction. She smiles frequently but it never reaches her eyes. She signed the survey permit the party finds in Phase 1 — their first breadcrumb leading to the conspiracy.

Armor Class 15 (studded leather)

Hit Points 78 (12d8 + 24)

Speed 30 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
11 (+0)16 (+3)14 (+2)18 (+4)16 (+3)18 (+4)

Saving Throws Dex +6, Int +7

Skills Deception +10 (expertise), Stealth +9 (expertise), Perception +6, Persuasion +7, Insight +6

Senses passive Perception 16

Languages Common, Elvish, Dwarvish, Thieves’ Cant

Challenge 8 (3,900 XP)

Traits

Assassinate. During her first turn, Lucretia has advantage on attack rolls against any creature that hasn’t taken a turn yet. Any hit against a surprised creature is a critical hit.

Sneak Attack (4d6). Once per turn, 14 (4d6) extra damage with a finesse/ranged weapon when she has advantage or an ally within 5 ft.

Preservation Society Director. Lucretia manages the infrastructure of Valerius’s operation — the surveillance, the stasis pods, the public relations. She signed the survey permit in Phase 1.

Actions

Shortsword. Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) piercing damage plus 7 (2d6) poison damage.

DM Notes

Lucretia is the campaign’s secondary villain and in many ways more hateful than Valerius. Where Valerius is a tragic philosopher, Lucretia is a cold pragmatist who enjoys her power. She manages the stasis pod operation personally, choosing who gets frozen and who gets “preserved.” Her Assassinate feature means her opening attack is devastating: if she surprises a target, the first hit is an automatic critical dealing 2d6 + 3 piercing + 4d6 poison + 8d6 Sneak Attack = average 45 damage in a single strike. She fights dirty — targeting healers first, using poison, and retreating when outmatched. She has 2d4 Golden Guard Veterans as bodyguards in most encounters. Her office at Heroes’ Rest contains the evidence needed to turn Captain Vayne. Sable has a personal vendetta against her.

biography:
Captain Aldric Vayne

Medium humanoid (human), lawful neutral

Captain of the Golden Guard, Valerius’s military commander. A duty-bound soldier who follows orders but has growing doubts. He is tall, broad-shouldered, with a close-cropped beard going grey at the temples and tired eyes that have seen too much. He wears immaculate gold-trimmed plate armor and carries a longsword named “Oath.” He can be turned to the party’s side with evidence from the Hall of Statues or testimony from freed champions.

Armor Class 18 (plate)

Hit Points 112 (15d8 + 45)

Speed 30 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
18 (+4)14 (+2)16 (+3)10 (+0)12 (+1)14 (+2)

Saving Throws Str +8, Con +7

Skills Athletics +8, Intimidation +6, Perception +5

Senses passive Perception 15

Languages Common

Challenge 9 (5,000 XP)

Traits

Improved Critical. Vayne scores a critical hit on a roll of 19 or 20.

Remarkable Athlete. Adds half proficiency bonus (rounded up) to any Strength, Dexterity, or Constitution check that doesn’t already use his proficiency bonus.

Indomitable (2/Day). Vayne can reroll a saving throw that he fails.

Commander of the Golden Guard. Vayne leads the Archduke’s elite soldiers. He can be turned against Valerius if presented with undeniable proof. If turned, half the Golden Guard joins the party.

Actions

Multiattack. Vayne makes three longsword attacks.

Longsword. Melee Weapon Attack: +8 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 8 (1d8 + 4) slashing damage.

Heavy Crossbow. Ranged Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, range 100/400 ft., one target. Hit: 9 (1d10 + 4) piercing damage.

DM Notes

Captain Vayne is the campaign’s most important “turn” opportunity. He is a good man serving a bad cause because he believes in order and stability. He follows Valerius because the Archduke brought peace to the Tri-Kingdoms — but he doesn’t know about the stasis pods or the frozen champions. Evidence that can turn him: the stasis pods beneath Solhaven Palace, testimony from the Iron Vanguard (especially Bron, whom Vayne respected), records from Lucretia’s office showing the scale of the operation, or the Hall of Statues showing frozen civilians. Turning Vayne requires a Persuasion check (DC 18 with evidence, DC 25 without). If turned, half the Golden Guard (those loyal to Vayne personally) switch sides — roughly 40 soldiers. This dramatically changes the final battle. In combat, Vayne is a straightforward but extremely effective fighter: three attacks at +8 with Improved Critical means he crits roughly 19% of the time. Indomitable (2/day) makes him very hard to take out with save-or-suck spells. He fights with honor and will accept surrender.
